narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 36, close to the standard 36.70, you've built an impressive academic foundation that many students would envy. Your Year 1 school decile of 1st place stands out exceptionally, far surpassing the average 6th decile, while your Year 3 decile of 2nd is also strong but slightly below the usual 5th or 6th. Coming from a middle-income family and planning full-time studies aligns with the norm for most applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 40.9% chance you'll fail the bar—high enough to trigger rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass on their first try. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your LSAT score, Year 3 school decile, and undergraduate GPA, ranked by their influence. Your LSAT, being right around average, worked strongly in your favor, nudging the model toward expecting bar passage much like it does for similar scorers. Likewise, your undergraduate GPA pulled convincingly toward success, reflecting how higher GPAs typically correlate with bar passers. But your Year 3 decile had the opposite effect, pushing hardest toward a failure prediction—likely because, even at 2nd place, it lags behind top performers who dominate bar success in the model's view of historical data. Compared to typical students, your profile shines in early years and college but shows a relative dip later, which tipped the scales despite your full-time commitment and background fitting the majority. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer—models like this capture patterns, not destinies. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT for a boost or strengthening recent academics through post-bac courses, then reapply to schools with more flexible thresholds; many successful lawyers overcome early hurdles just like this.
